elements.section-2411-003: Background on fake weighted projective spaces
elements.sent-2411-128: A fake weighted projective space is a $\QQ$-factorial, projective toric variety of Picard number one.
elements.sent-2411-129: The defining data of these varieties are projective generator matrices that means integral $n \times (n+1)$ matrices \begin{equation}P \ = \ [v_0, \ldots, v_n]\end{equation} with pairwise distinct primitive columns $v_0, \ldots, v_n \in \ZZ^n$ generating $\QQ^n$ as a convex cone.
elements.sent-2411-130: With $P$ we associate the unique fan $\Sigma(P)$ in $\ZZ^n$ having the maximal cones \begin{equation}\sigma_i \ := \ \cone(v_j; \ j \ne i), \quad i = 0, \ldots, n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-131: The fake weighted projective space $Z(P)$ is the toric variety defined by $\Sigma(P)$.
elements.sent-2411-132: Up to isomorphy, we obtain every fake weighted projective space this way.
elements.sent-2411-133: Given projective generator matrices $P,P'$, one has $Z(P) \cong Z(P')$ if and only if $P' = S \cdot P \cdot T$ with a unimodular matrix $S$ and a permutation matrix~$T$.

elements.section-2411-006: $\KK^*$-surfaces of Picard number one and integral degree
elements.sent-2411-559: We explicitly describe all quasismooth projective rational $\KK^*$-surfaces of Picard number one and integral degree; see Theorems~\ref{thm:adjpartner and~\ref{thm:thm4 , covering in particular Theorem~\ref{thm:introthm3 from the introduction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-560: An important step on the way is Proposition~\ref{prop:P1toP , showing, roughly speaking, that a fake weighted projective plane is an equivariant limit of a quasismooth $\KK^*$-surface of the same Picard number and degree provided that at least one of its toric fixed points is a $T$-singularity.}
elements.sent-2411-561: The second main ingredient is the study of the singularities of the fake weighted projective planes performed in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-562: We first recall the necessary background on $\KK^*$-surfaces of Picard number one; see~\cite[Sec.~4]{HaHaHaSp and \cite{HaHaSp for more details.}}
elements.sent-2411-563: These come embedded into certain fake weighted projective spaces.
elements.sent-2411-564: More precisely, consider an integral $3 \times 4$ projective generator matrix of the shape \begin{equation}P \ = \ \left[ {rrrr} -1 -1 l_1 0 -1 -1 0 l_2 0 d_0 d_1 d_2  \right], \qquad {c} \scriptstyle 1 \le d_1 \le l_1 \le l_2, \ \ \gcd(l_i,d_i) = 1, [3pt] \scriptstyle d_0 + \frac{d_1}{l_1} + \frac{d_2}{l_2} \ < \ 0 \ < \ \frac{d_1}{l_1} + \frac{d_2}{l_2}. \end{equation} Let $Z = Z(P)$ be the associated fake weighted projective space.
elements.formula-2411-124: \begin{equation}P \ = \ \left[ {rrrr} -1 -1 l_1 0 -1 -1 0 l_2 0 d_0 d_1 d_2  \right], \qquad {c} \scriptstyle 1 \le d_1 \le l_1 \le l_2, \ \ \gcd(l_i,d_i) = 1, [3pt] \scriptstyle d_0 + \frac{d_1}{l_1} + \frac{d_2}{l_2} \ < \ 0 \ < \ \frac{d_1}{l_1} + \frac{d_2}{l_2}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-565: Observe that the fake weight vector of $Z$ is given as \begin{equation}w \ = \ w(P) \ = \ (-l_1l_2d_0 - l_2d_1 - l_1d_2, \, l_2d_1 + l_1d_2, \, - l_2d_0, \, -l_1d_0) \ \in \ \ZZ_{>0}^4.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-566: Now, denote by $S_1,S_2,S_3$ the coordinates on the acting torus $\TT^3 \subseteq Z$.
elements.sent-2411-567: Then we obtain a projective surface \begin{equation}X \ := \ X(P) \ := \ \overline{V(1+S_1+S_2)} \ \subseteq \ Z,\end{equation} taking the closure of $V(1+S_1+S_2) \subseteq \TT^3$ in $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-568: The multiplicative group $\KK^*$ acts on $X$ as a subtorus of the torus $\TT^3 \subseteq Z(P)$ via \begin{equation}t \cdot s \ = \ (s_1,s_2,ts_3).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-569: The $\KK^*$-surface $X$ is projective, rational, quasismooth, del Pezzo and of Picard number one.
elements.sent-2411-570: In homogeneous coordinates on $Z$, we have \begin{equation}X \ = \ V(T_1T_2 + T_3^{l_1} + T_4^{l_2}) \ \subseteq \ Z.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-571: Moreover, $X$ is non-toric if and only if $l_1,l_2 > 1$.
elements.sent-2411-572: There are precisely three $\KK^*$-fixed points on $X$; in homogeneous coordinates they are given as \begin{equation}x_0 \ = \ [0,0,\zeta,1], \ \zeta^{l_1} = -1, \qquad x_1 \ = \ [0,1,0,0], \qquad x_2 \ = \ [1,0,0,0].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-573: The fixed point $x_0$ is hyperbolic and $x_1,x_2$ are both elliptic.
elements.sent-2411-574: There are exactly two non-trivial orbits $\KK^* \cdot z_1$ and $\KK^* \cdot z_2$ with non-trivial isotropy groups: \begin{equation}z_1 = [-1,1,0,1], \quad \vert \KK^*_{z_1} \vert = l_1, \qquad\qquad z_2 = [-1,1,1,0], \quad \vert \KK^*_{z_2} \vert = l_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-575: Moreover, for the local class group orders of the three $\KK^*$-fixed points $x_0,x_1,x_2 \in X$, we obtain \begin{equation}\cl(x_0) = -d_0, \qquad \cl(x_1) = w_2, \qquad \cl(x_2) = w_1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-576: Finally, the self intersection number of the canonical divisor $\mathcal{K _X$ on $X$ can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}K_X^2 \ = \ \Bigl( \frac{1}{w_1} + \frac{1}{w_2} \Bigr) \Bigl(2 + \frac{l_1}{l_2} + \frac{l_2}{l_1} \Bigr) \ = \ \frac{\cl(x_0)}{\cl(x_1)\cl(x_2)} (l_1+l_2)^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-577: As a consequence of \cite[Thm.~4.18, Props.~4.9, 4.15 and~5.1]{HaHaHaSp , every non-toric, quasismooth, rational, projective $\KK^*$-surface of Picard number one is isomorphic to an $X(P)$ as above.}
